subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Consistently clear and accurate communication of the research focus throughout the report
Unambiguous, well-focused research question effectively integrated into every section
Comprehensive and well-justified methodology with appropriate secondary data sources and reflective source appraisal
weaknesses: Introduction delays citation of empirical SSRI data and does not immediately contextualise the RQ with evidence
Search protocol (databases, keywords, inclusion/exclusion) is not fully specified, limiting replicability
Conclusion is descriptive and does not succinctly summarise the direct comparison of SSRIs versus CBT against the RQ
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Focus and Method fall squarely in the top band (5â6). The student sustains an appropriate, sharply defined focus, formulates the RQ with precision, and deploys a coherent, well-rationalised methodology. Minor lapses in procedural specificity and the introductory pacing do not undermine the overall excellence of this criterion, justifying the maximum mark of 6.

strengths: Generally accurate use of key psychological and statistical terminology (e.g., remission rate, KaplanâMeier curves)
Effective integration of empirical findings from peer-reviewed trials and meta-analyses
Clear presentation of pharmacological concepts and defined evaluation benchmarks (response vs remission rates)
weaknesses: Occasional oversimplifications and scattered imprecisions (e.g., conflation of BPD subtypes, mislabelled confidence intervals)
Reliance on tertiary sources (e.g., Wikipedia, popular blogs) undermines scholarly rigor in places
Some justifications (transferring MDD cultural data to IBPD) lack methodological support
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding is solid but not flawless. Terminology and concepts are applied correctly for the most part, and source application is good, yet inconsistencies and occasional reliance on non-scholarly references prevent this from reaching the top band. A score of 4 accurately reflects consistent competence with room for greater precision.

strengths: Robust selection of recent, peer-reviewed trials, meta-analyses and institutional reviews directly relevant to the RQ
Nuanced analysis of quantitative findings (odds ratios, survival analysis) to compare treatment efficacy
Structured, reasoned argument with critique of cultural bias, confirmation bias and methodological variability
weaknesses: Occasional causal assertions (e.g., lifestyle factors causing depression) lack evidential support
Statistical results (e.g., global odds ratios, A&E trend reversals) are noted but not fully interpreted
Brief mentions of theoretical perspectives (psychoanalytic theory) are underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Critical Thinking align with the 4â6 band: the student exhibits clear, mostly well-supported analysis and a reasoned evaluative discussion. The depth and coherence of critical engagement warrant the top mark within this band (6), even though the work falls short of the consistently sophisticated synthesis required for higher bands.

strengths: Adheres fully to structural conventions (title page, contents, numbered sections, captions, cross-references)
Layout, formatting and citation style are applied consistently and correctly for the most part
Effective use of visuals (CONSORT diagram, outcome charts) with clear captions enhances readability
weaknesses: Abstract or executive summary is missing
Proofreading oversights (incomplete sentences, verbose section headers, mid-sentence cut-offs) detract slightly from professionalism
Occasional repetitive phrasing and paragraph length could be refined for clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets all the criteria for the upper band (3â4): the report is well-structured, formatted and navigable, with minor cosmetic and proofreading issues. Awarding the maximum mark of 4 recognizes the overall high quality of the reportâs presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Accuracy and consistency.** Key psychological and statistical termsââremission rate,â âKaplanâMeier curve,â âconfirmation bias,â âTAU,â âsomatic anxietyâ â are used correctly, and the student distinguishes between subtypes of BPD and related constructs such as MDD.

**Limitations.** There are moments of oversimplification (e.g., equating absence of symptoms with absence of disorder) and scattered inaccuracies (heritability percentage ambiguities, conflation of BPD subtypes). In places, terminology slips (âchemical unevennessâ) and referencing of psychoanalytic theory is brief and somewhat superficial.

**Judgement.** Overall, terminology use is mostly accurate and concepts are generally well handled, but consistency and precision are not impeccable. This situates the strand in the upper part of the 3-4 band, so a score of 4 is awarded.

**Formatting conventions.** The student provides a word count, title, page numbers, section headers and a comprehensive Works Cited list in near-consistent APA style. Visuals are labelled and referenced, and the table of contents links accurately to page numbers.

**Minor inconsistencies.** A few reference entries omit volume/issue data or italics; occasional bold/HTML tags appear in the main text, and spacing/line-break anomalies occur where markdown code is visible. Nonetheless, these issues are cosmetic and do not significantly hinder clarity or professionalism.

**Overall assessment.** Layout and formatting considerations are clearly applied and largely correct, satisfying the upper tier (3-4) descriptor. A score of 4 is therefore appropriate.

**Demonstrated knowledge.** The student explains neurochemical action of SSRIs, CBT principles, and IBPD symptomatology with generally secure understanding, integrating empirical findings (e.g., remission/response rates, KaplanâMeier interpretation).

**Source application.** Peer-reviewed trials are summarised accurately, with tables and figures aiding comprehension. However, occasional reliance on non-scholarly or tertiary sources (Wikipedia, popular blogs) and some misstatements about BPD subtypes and genetic percentages reveal gaps in precision. Application of MDD data to IBPD is acknowledged but not always fully justified methodologically.

**Overall fit.** The essay shows good but not consistently excellent command of psychological knowledge and literature; therefore the work best aligns with the 3-4 band, meriting a score of 4.

**Breadth and relevance.** The student assembles a solid corpus of clinical trials, meta-analyses and authoritative reviews directly examining SSRIs, CBT and related outcomes; most are recent, peer-reviewed and clearly germane to the RQ. Non-scholarly inclusions are few and acknowledged.

**Critical appraisal.** Source limitations (e.g., small effect sizes, unclear TAU components, cultural bias in depression diagnosis) are noted, showing an intermediate level of critique. However, screening procedures and author credibility checks remain lightly described, and some extrapolations from MDD data to IBPD lack rigorous justification.

**Overall alignment.** The research base is mostly pertinent and of good quality, meeting the descriptors for the 4-6 band; depth is uneven, preventing elevation to the top 10-12 band. A band score of 6 within this range is appropriate.

**Clarity and precision.** The research question is stated word-for-word in the title, introduction and approach sections, showing unambiguous formulation. Its comparative nature (âSSRIs versus CBTâ) gives a sharply defined focus that guides source selection, analysis and conclusion.

**Integration with discussion.** Each empirical study is explicitly introduced as evidence addressing either pharmacological or psychotherapeutic efficacy, and the evaluation sections repeatedly reference how findings bear upon the RQ (e.g., KaplanâMeier curves for CBT+TAU vs TAU, antidepressant vs placebo meta-analytic figures).

**Sustained alignment.** Even when the essay explores genetic underpinnings or cultural diagnostic issues, the student explicitly links these factors to potential impacts on treatment effectiveness, demonstrating an effective, ongoing connection between question and discussion characteristic of the top band.

**Organisational clarity.** The essay adheres to EE structural conventions: title page, abstract substitute, table of contents, sequentially numbered sections (Introduction, Approach, Evaluation, Conclusion, Works Cited). Visuals are embedded with captions and cross-referenced, aiding navigation.

**Cohesion and flow.** Paragraphing is logical, headings are used effectively, and transitions between sections are generally smooth. Minor lapses (occasional long quotations, slightly repetitive headings) do not impede overall readability.

**Verdict.** Given that all required elements are present and appropriately arranged, the structure fully meets the expectations of the 3-4 band; the higher value of 4 is merited.

**Structured argument.** The essay progresses logically from background, through methodological justification, to comparative evaluation and conclusion, creating a reasoned argumentative thread. Each study is weighed for strengths and limitations, and implications for the RQ are spelled out.

**Evaluation quality.** The student critiques cultural bias, confirmation bias, sample attrition, and unclear TAU definitions, demonstrating partial critical engagement. However, evaluation occasionally halts at identifying a flaw without fully analysing its impact on conclusions, and some alternative explanations (e.g., generalising MDD data) are noted but not deeply interrogated.

**Overall performance.** The discussion shows clear reasoning and some critical reflection, aligning with the 4-6 band. Consistency and depth are adequate though not exceptional, justifying a score of 6.

annotations.comment: The sentence ending with âare a known approachâ is incomplete, suggesting an editing oversight; complete and proofread for clarity and coherence.
The distinction between CBT and broader behavioural therapies is misinterpreted; consult original NIH definitions or authoritative psychotherapy reviews to clarify methodological scope.
The table of contents is comprehensive and accurately formatted, listing all major sections with correct page references to guide reader navigation.
Repetition of the research question in the header reinforces sustained alignment with the investigation focus, ensuring the RQ remains central throughout the report.
Selecting sources from .gov, .edu, and .org domains demonstrates informed source evaluation, prioritizing reliability and authority in data collection.
Reflection on IB course relevance is descriptive; deepen discussion by linking personal insights to conceptual frameworks or research skills development.
Works cited entries display inconsistent APA conventions and missing italics; standardise formatting (authors, titles, journal names) for professionalism.
Using Wikipedia as a source undermines scholarly reliability; replace tertiary references with peer-reviewed primary literature whenever possible.

The title is communicated accurately and prominently, establishing a clear and coherent focus on comparing SSRIs versus CBT for IBPD, which effectively orients the reader to the research purpose and scope.
The citation âSchwartz, 2022â is not listed in the works cited, indicating an incomplete reference list; verify and include all sources to ensure proper attribution.
The claim that lifestyle factors directly cause depression lacks empirical support; either provide citations for these assertions or adjust language to reflect correlation rather than causation.
The word count is clearly stated at the beginning of the document, meeting the formatting requirement and demonstrating adherence to IB Extended Essay guidelines.
Stating the expected outcome (SSRI+CBT superiority) risks introducing confirmation bias; preserve objectivity by framing hypotheses as research possibilities rather than certainties.
Incorporating a cross-cultural study of Malay and Australian depression presentations demonstrates depth in considering contextual moderators of treatment effectiveness.
Confidence interval notation is mislabelled as P; revise to standard format (e.g., 95% CI [lower, upper]) for statistical accuracy.
The Kaplan-Meier survival plots are well integrated and enhance the depth of analysis by visualizing time-to-event outcomes.
Questioning the consistency of TAU across patients exhibits strong methodological scrutiny and highlights potential variability in treatment comparisons.
Defining response and remission rates as evaluation criteria establishes clear, measurable benchmarks for comparing treatment efficacy.
The introductory evaluation paragraph does not immediately cite empirical SSRI data, delaying the connection between criteria and evidence; integrate data sooner for coherence.
Applying MDD cultural data to IBPD without clear justification overstates transferability; clarify which constructs validly map onto the IBPD context.
The brief mention of psychoanalytic theory is underdeveloped; expand theoretical evaluation or integrate it more thoroughly into the discussion.
The personal code is included without the authorâs name, which is unconventional; consider including a brief author identifier or omitting the code to maintain report personalization.
An abstract or executive summary is missing from the contents; consider adding a concise overview of key findings to strengthen structural conventions.
The introduction defines IBPD but lacks explicit justification for comparing SSRIs and CBT, reducing the immediacy of the research purpose; contextualise the problem gap more clearly.
Key terminology such as âheritable rateâ and âsymptom alleviationâ is used accurately, demonstrating solid subject knowledge and appropriate concept application.
The methodology section clearly states the use of secondary data and outlines ethical and logistical justifications, providing a sound rationale for research design.
Listing specific SSRIs (Citalopram, Escitalopram, Fluoxetine) demonstrates familiarity with pharmacological options and strengthens the empirical foundation of the comparison.
Databases, search terms, and inclusion/exclusion criteria are not specified, limiting replicability; consider adding a clear search protocol to enhance methodological transparency.
Introducing biomarkers and genetic profiling considerations reflects advanced critical thinking about personalised treatment strategies.
The transition to the Boschloo et al. meta-analysis is abrupt; add a linking sentence to improve narrative flow between sections.
Critical appraisal of the cultural studyâs imposed etic approach and memory biases shows strong analytical engagement with research limitations.
Identifying confirmation bias in diagnosis reflects advanced critical thinking about clinician decision-making and its impact on treatment validity.
Section headers are verbose and repetitive; consider concise titles to improve document flow and reader orientation.
The 96% follow-up rate is reported twice; streamline the text by consolidating repetitive information for readability.
Including the CONSORT diagram with a clear caption adheres to presentation conventions and effectively illustrates trial flow.
Interpretation of the global odds ratio is minimal; discuss what values of 1.04 and 0.86 imply for relative treatment effectiveness.
Detailed description of TAU components and follow-up rates enhances transparency in trial evaluation and source application.
The reversal of A&E trends under CBT+TAU is noted but not explored; analyze potential clinical or behavioral explanations for this pattern.
The visual diagram illustrating antidepressant versus placebo outcomes reinforces comprehension and meets presentation conventions.
Correct reporting of confidence intervals and log-rank P-values demonstrates quantitative literacy in survival analysis interpretation.
Lengthy paragraphs on medication effects reduce readability; break into focused subsections or bullet points to improve clarity.
The comparative summary of placebo versus antidepressant response rates in moderate-severe depression is concise and effectively quantifies treatment benefit.
Reliance on tertiary sources (e.g., non-peer-reviewed health websites) weakens scholarly rigor; corroborate with primary research where possible.
The citation âExploring your mind, 2022â appears in text but is missing from the works cited; ensure consistency by including all referenced sources.
Detailed reporting of sample demographics and treatment allocation (SSRI vs CBT) provides clear context for interpreting meta-analytic findings.
The nuanced analysis of SSRIsâ direct and indirect effects on specific symptom clusters demonstrates deep conceptual understanding of treatment mechanisms.
The conclusion statement is cut off mid-sentence, indicating a proofreading oversight; ensure all conclusions are fully articulated.
The symptom categorization list is presented as bullets in narrative text; consider converting it into a concise table for academic clarity.
The concluding discussion is descriptive and does not succinctly summarise how SSRIs and CBT compare against the RQ; strengthen by directly answering the RQ.
